serving.natural_language_portion: A typical serving is approximately 30 g of dry atta flour, equivalent to the amount used to make one medium chapati or roti.

nutrient_source.title: Atta (Whole Wheat Flour) — Singapore HPB Foodo Database
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Values taken directly from the HPB Foodo entry for atta flour (whole wheat flour). All per-100g macros and micronutrients are sourced from this record with confidence 0.95.

gi_source.title: Chapati made from atta flour — Glycemic Index Database
gi_source.source_match_notes: GI of 62 is based on the University of Sydney GI Database entry for chapati/roti made from atta (whole wheat) flour, which is the primary end-use form of this ingredient. Dry atta flour itself is not directly tested; the chapati preparation is the closest approved analogous entry. GL per serving calculated as (62 × 71.97 × 0.30) / 100 = 13.4 g for a 30 g dry-flour serving.

hypertension.key_factor: Very low sodium (2 mg/100 g) and high potassium (363 mg/100 g) with excellent dietary fibre (10.7 g/100 g)
hypertension.guidance: Atta flour is strongly aligned with DASH diet principles due to its negligible sodium content, high potassium-to-sodium ratio, and high dietary fibre; individuals with hypertension can use it freely provided salt is not added during dough preparation.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: High total carbohydrates (72 g/100 g) moderated by high dietary fibre (10.7 g/100 g), medium GI of 62, and substantial protein (13.2 g/100 g)
type2_diabetes.guidance: Atta flour has a medium GI of approximately 62 and high dietary fibre that significantly slows glucose absorption compared to refined white flour; individuals with Type 2 Diabetes should practice strict portion control, limiting chapati to one or two thin pieces per meal to manage postprandial glycaemic response.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Low total fat (2.5 g/100 g), low saturated fat (0.43 g/100 g), zero cholesterol, high dietary fibre (10.7 g/100 g), and high potassium (363 mg/100 g)
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Atta flour's high dietary fibre content supports LDL cholesterol reduction, its negligible saturated fat and zero cholesterol pose no cardiovascular risk, and its potassium and magnesium content supports vascular health; it is a heart-healthy staple when prepared without added fats such as ghee or oil.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Whole-grain food with high dietary fibre supporting gut microbiome health and immune modulation; low in saturated fat with no trans fat
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Atta flour is generally suitable for individuals with rheumatoid arthritis as a whole-grain, low-saturated-fat staple; however, those with co-occurring wheat sensitivity or gluten intolerance should monitor symptoms, and deep-fried preparations such as puri should be avoided due to pro-inflammatory advanced glycation end-products.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Atta flour is a finely milled whole wheat flour originating from the Indian subcontinent, where it is the primary staple flour used to make unleavened flatbreads including chapati, roti, paratha, and puri. Unlike refined white flour, atta retains the bran and germ of the wheat kernel, giving it a fine brown colour, slightly nutty aroma, and smooth yet slightly grainy texture. It is produced by stone-grinding or roller-milling whole durum or semi-hard wheat varieties. Nutritionally, atta flour is a dense source of complex carbohydrates (72 g/100 g) and is distinguished from refined flour by its exceptional dietary fibre content (10.7 g/100 g), which slows glucose absorption and supports digestive health. It provides substantial protein (13.2 g/100 g), moderate iron (3.6 mg/100 g), high potassium (363 mg/100 g), and meaningful B-vitamins including thiamine, niacin, and B6. Its sodium content is negligible at 2 mg/100 g. With a medium glycemic index of approximately 62, atta flour raises blood glucose more slowly than refined white flour, making it a preferred staple for individuals managing Type 2 Diabetes when portion-controlled. It is widely consumed across South Asian communities in Singapore, Malaysia, and globally, and is a cornerstone ingredient in Indian, Pakistani, and Bangladeshi cuisines. Atta flour is naturally vegan, halal, and kosher, but contains gluten and is unsuitable for individuals with coeliac disease or wheat allergy.

What is the glycemic index of atta flour?

Is atta flour suitable for hypertension?
How much protein is in atta flour?

ingredient_synonyms.atta: whole wheat flour used in South Asian flatbreads
ingredient_synonyms.chapati flour: atta flour used specifically for making chapati
ingredient_synonyms.roti flour: atta flour used for making roti
ingredient_synonyms.gehun ka atta: Hindi term for whole wheat flour
ingredient_synonyms.whole wheat flour (Indian): atta flour as described in Western contexts

confidence_reason: Nutrient values sourced directly from the HPB Singapore Foodo database with confidence 0.95. GI estimated from the University of Sydney GI Database using chapati (atta-based) as the closest approved analogous entry. All core macros, secondary nutrients, and micronutrients are supported by approved primary sources.
notes: Saturated fat (0.43 g), monounsaturated fat (0.28 g), and polyunsaturated fat (1.05 g) values are sourced from USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy entry for whole wheat flour (FDC ID 20080) as the HPB record does not disaggregate fatty acid fractions; these are consistent with the total fat of 2.5 g/100 g reported by HPB. Trans fat is reported as 0.0 g (confirmed zero per USDA FoodData Central for whole wheat flour). Vitamin B12 is 0.0 g — naturally zero as atta flour is a plant-derived product containing no animal-sourced B12. Cholesterol is 0.0 mg — naturally zero as atta flour is a plant-based food. Vitamin C and vitamin D are set to -1 as these are not meaningfully present in whole wheat flour and are not reported in the HPB or USDA records for this food. Vitamin A is set to -1 as whole wheat flour contains negligible preformed vitamin A and the HPB record does not report this value. GI of 62 is an analogous estimate based on the University of Sydney GI Database chapati entry; dry atta flour is not directly GI-tested. GL per serving = (62 × 71.97 × 0.30) / 100 = 13.4 for a 30 g dry-flour serving. Atta flour contains gluten and is not suitable for individuals with coeliac disease or wheat allergy. The food is classified as South Asian / Indian cuisine; multilingual names are provided in Chinese (simplified and traditional), Malay, Tamil, and Hindi to reflect the Singapore multicultural context where atta flour is widely used.
